sentences.text: Henryka Shaw.
H-E-N-R-Y-K-A S-H-A-W.
Schermant.
S-C-H-E-R-M-A-N-T.
About 60-something-- eight, nine.
Nine, I think.
It is.
In Cracow, Podgorze.
Poland.
No, it was not.
It was rather very much non-Jewish area.
Podgorze was beyond the bridge, and that was not a Jewish area.
Jewish area was more in the heart of Cracow.
My father was a technical engineer.
He had an office in the city.
And he used to import parts of machinery from Germany.
Yes, I did.
I had a sister-- I have a sister-- and a brother I had who perished in Russia.
My sister is older-- six years older than I am.
And my brother was four years older.
I was the youngest.
Very sedate, very much [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] ordinary.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We had traditional holidays.
We have kept traditional Jewish holidays.
We all went to school.
My mother used to go to the office to help my father.
Otherwise she stayed at home-- prepared the dinner.
We used to have dinner in the-- 2:00 or so.
Everyone went to school only half a day, not like it is in Australia.
So she always was home for us when we returned from school.
Not really, no.
I would say we were very assimilated.
We had very Polish names.
And my father was very assimilated Jew.
But we did have religious holidays.
We observed religious holidays.
All of them.
All of them.
In particular, Passover, which we had now-- the whole family would come to us, because my father was the oldest brother.
We would open the doors of another room, and there was a table going right through.
And everybody-- aunties and uncles and their children-- we were all together.
Well, fairly big.
My auntie had a son.
She was separated from her husband.
There was another uncle-- a younger brother with a wife and three children.
We were very often together, especially on holidays.
My two aunties lived in the same house.
It was a family house belonging to us.
I even remember my grandmother was living in the same house.
And unfortunately, she passed away before the war.
But I do have recollection of her stature and the way she spoke.
Very much like my father-- tall, very distinguished-looking lady.
Yes, my brother did have bar mitzvah.
Very vaguely, I remember it was celebrated in temple.
It was very close to Hebrew Gymnasium where he used to attend.
And so did my sister, and she completed Hebrew Gymnasium before the war.
That's where we celebrated his bar mitzvah.
I went to school in Podgorze.
First few years I went to a school called Jadwiga Dabrowska-- Dabrowski-- it was called Dabrowski school.
And later I went to Henryk Sienkiewicz school, in Podgorze, very close by.
Well, the first school was very pleasant, very small, and I got on very well with the teachers.
I liked them, and they liked me.
And, I would say, because of my height, they very often allowed me to look after the class while they were busy in office or doing something, which sort of pleased me.
But later on, when I went to Henryk Sienkiewicz school, I have less pleasant memories.
Well, one of the school teachers-- it was a very impersonal school-- larger.
And the girls were less known to me.
The school was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] close to where we live, and yet perhaps one or two girls I was friendly with, and I did miss the friends from the first school.
At one stage, a teacher-- her name was Miss [INAUDIBLE]-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] during the History class, made a statement that Jews are [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bastards.
Her expression was [? dranie, ?] which I feel this is more or less the same.
To that I have stood up, and spoke up for the Jews.
I was very Jewish.
And I made a point, telling her that non-Jews can also be not so very nice.
So it had nothing to do with the religion.
But, of course, a great discussion evolved in the class.
And I was very provoked.
All sorts of things were said by the girls and the teacher.
And I saw I was losing the--
The usual thing, that the Jews were not very good, and they were exploiting their mothers.
Their mothers worked for the Jews.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was very annoying, because I felt my mother also had help at home, and she has access to anything and everything.
She was treated like family.
And I felt that everybody else did similarly.
And if they didn't, it's because they weren't very nice people, but not that they were Jews.
So I said a little ditty, which was not very pleasant and not very nice.
But I was totally provoked.
Oh, I remember very well.
I'm just embarrassed to tell you. 'Who doesn't care for Jews can kiss their bottom.'
But it rhymed very well in Polish.
And it wasn't a pleasant expression.
Absolutely flabbergasted that I dared to say something like that.
And so was I.
I didn't think I would come out with something like that.
But I was totally provoked.
And I was told to take my school case and go home and to bring my parents.
I came home.
I had to tell my parents, because otherwise I wouldn't be able to go back to school.
I remember I came with my parents, both of them, to the headmistress's office.
And I was sitting at the back.
Nobody asked me anything.
I wasn't allowed to say a word.
I couldn't put my point of view.
And my parents were only concerned that I should be allowed to come back to school.
I remember I never went back to that school, or any other school, for that matter.
That was the end of my formal education.
The war came, 6-- 6 or 9 of September-- when was it, exactly?
In September, '39.
I would say about 13.
Well, certainly not the way I have spoken up.
One didn't speak like that.
Today, you say all those words and lots of other four-letter words.
But not in Poland before the war, 50-odd years ago.
It was a disaster.
I certainly would have been expelled from school and any other school.
I wouldn't have access to school-- certainly not in Poland.
It was a Tisha B'av.
Tisha B'av is a holiday, Jewish holiday, that everybody cries because the temple was destroyed.
And that's how they reacted.
Nobody asked me-- nor my mother, nor my father asked me.
They probably understood that I was justified, but they were disappointed that I used that language.
Because that was the worst I knew then.
And they didn't think that their daughter would speak like that, certainly not in the class, to the teacher.
Oh, very nice.
[LAUGHS] No, I was very timid.
Very shy.
I have been always very tall, very thin, not particularly good-looking-- at least that's what I thought.
Some people agreed with me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Lank, poorly developed-- no bust, no-- no figure.
So I was really inhibited.
I really was.
That was something that-- everybody was surprised that I dared to out up with something like that.
So where did that come from?
Oh, we live in that area, that the language was used like that.
But not in school.
Not in front of the teachers.
So it was a common word to use.
Even my children and grandchildren in Australia know that word.
It's a lovely word.
You know, like, little 'botty'?
It's a nice, sweet word-- not-- not so terrible.
But then, it was.
And certainly to tell that, and almost to tell the teacher.
Because I included all.
Oh, no, there were lots of Jewish girls.
But none of them stood up and said anything.
None of them have taken part in the discussion.
I even expected the religious teacher, that he would participate in that problem and help me get back to school, because I felt I was totally provoked.
But, of course, his salary depended on the school management, and it was public school.
So he wouldn't have said anything.
That was the general attitude that we have put up with anything and everything-- kept quiet.
Never-- to any antisemitic remark, we would never respond.
The procedure was to hide, to run away.
If stones were thrown, you said, 'thank you' and you ran, and glad that the stone didn't hit you.
And if it did, too bad.
But I wasn't brought up that way.
My father was also six-footer-- six foot two, and I could see that he stood up for himself.
So I  had a role model.
Even if they thought I didn't receive all that-- I didn't perceive everything what was going on at home-- I absorbed everything.
My parents spoke very often German because they were, during the war, under Austrian-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] under Austrian government.
So, especially if they didn't want us to understand, they spoke German, and that helped us to learn the language.
Oh yes, constantly.
That was everyday occurrence-- here, there, everywhere.
But we sort of-- like on a duck's back.
It wasn't that important, unless it came close to the home-- it concerned me or concerned somebody close to me.
Well, that did hurt very much.
But that was nothing new.
Absolutely nothing new.
I wouldn't say I belonged, but I did go to many, because that was Saturday afternoon.
We had no television, and that was entertainment.
I would go to Bnei Akiva.
We were gathering on the river Wisla.
And there was a picnic.
And there were girls and boys.
And I would say I already sort of noticed boys.
And if that afternoon finished, then we went to Shomer Hatzair with another girlfriend, because the boys were already more grown-up, and that was a more serious organization.
And that I wasn't allowed to go to, because the boys were also older.
But I did.
I was tall, and I was accepted.
And the afternoon tea was good, too.
So we always sort of travelled from one-- there was Dror.
I remember occasionally I would go there, to see what's going there.
Actively I was too young, really, to belong.
And we had a lot of Judaism at home, so my parents did not particularly care that I do belong to any organization.
I did get the upbringing-- the Jewish upbringing-- at home.
My sister and my brother went to Hebrew Gymnasium, so I even had a smattering of Hebrew.
Today when I go to Israel, my friends who knew me from before the war are amazed how much Hebrew I understand and how much Hebrew I can speak.
So that was pleasant.
Pardon?
That's correct.

Oh, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember on the very day that war-- the Germans [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dropped bombs very close to us, on a bridge on which there were trains going with the soldiers travelling.
I wouldn't know exactly which direction, because by the time I reached the bridge and saw what was happening, it was already-- the bridge-- the train was standing.
And there was a lot of blood and a lot of crying and a lot of dead people.
But my mother was a nurse during the First World War.
She took lots of sheets and tried to bandage them and helped them the best she knew how.
And later, it was cordoned off, and we were told to go.
And my mother always used to tell me, go home.
So I-- we did go home, eventually, and a proper ambulance came to help and to take them away.
But the Germans did a lot of damage on that very first day.
And the distance from my home to that bridge was perhaps half a kilometer-- not further than that.
So we saw the planes-- whoosh-- swooping down on the bridge.
We saw the bombs falling.
Terrified!
Terrified.
You know, I sort of-- I always had the attitude to accept these things.
Even then, as bad as it was, we accept it.
The next thing-- I remember that more planes came the same day, in different spots.
They were very well-informed where are the army barracks, where to bomb, what to bomb, and what not to bomb.
Actually, Cracow is a beautiful city.
It has not been destroyed.
There is a beautiful palace-- Wawel.
To this day, it's standing in its glory.
And the bridges were preserved, except that bridge on which the train was traveling.
It was important to them to destroy the soldiers rather than the bridge.
A few days later, maybe the following day, my parents decided that we've got to run, to get out of the city.
They thought that that will continue.
And everybody was told to take a few things into a little case.
And we went-- we ran.
We ran faster on foot than the Germans were approaching in trucks and in tanks.
We really ran.
It took a long time for them to catch up with us.
We went towards [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Russian border.
But our aim was to go to my grandparents, who lived very close to Przeworsk in a place called Siedleczka.
Yes.
S-I-E-D-L-E-C-Z-K-A.
And Przeworsk-- P-R-Z-E-W-O-R-S-K.
That's correct.
Yes.
And they lived deeper in Galicia.
And we thought, well, we will wait for whatever will happen down there.
We will have food.
Where in Podgorze, in Cracow, where we lived, you bought enough food for the day.
There were shops.
You did not have a storage to put things away, like one does here.
People have a cellar full of wine.
Well, we kept coal in the cellar.
And anyhow, nobody was buying flour and sugar in those quantities to preserve for months.
And we thought that that will take years, the war, so best we will be in the village.
The whole family.
My duty was to have a tiny little suitcase-- to carry that suitcase, which was full of chocolate-- [?
Suchard ?] and all sorts of chocolate.
In the house where we lived, my uncle had a konditorei.
So that was my duty.
And my poor parents always realized that I was left in the back because I kept opening the thing and nibbling at the chocolate.
I was still-- as tall as I was, I think I was very immature for my age, then.
Moments were that I understood everything what was going on.
But the attitude of my parents was not to share anything-- any problems-- with the children.
What I understood in German, or grasped a little bit from the Polish conversation, that I have known.
But not-- children, please come together.
We're going away.
We have to run so the Germans don't get at us.
None of that.
I just remember the-- the incidents that we have found ourselves going.
Where What for?
Nobody told us.
Only later I surmised that that's where we were heading-- to my grandparents.
How did you travel?
By foot, most of the time.
Occasionally my father begged somebody to allow me to go onto a horse-drawn wagon for an hour or two.
But I was very good at walking.
I didn't mind.
And it-- we were really-- as fast as we were trying to go, the amount of people that were going didn't allow us to go very fast.
Because there were cars, bicycles, and the whole road was taken.
So if you wanted to overtake somebody, you had to go into the ditch, go out, and be a few people ahead.
And that didn't get anywhere.
There were no trains.
Absolutely.
Every so often, we had to go into the woods or into the ditch to hide from the planes.
And they were bombing.
Unfortunately, if the horse was killed-- I think one was very concerned about those horses, because they have provided the means of transporting what everyone had.
We didn't have a carriage.
We didn't have a horse.
But we always managed to attach ourself to another carriage.
You know, you hold on to that wagon, and walk with it, and it's easier.
And you sort of acquired a certain speed.
And in the evening, we would go into the forest and try to sleep a little bit.
Occasionally, if somebody came with a torch, with a light, oh, that for sure was spy.
So it was a lot of that going on.
And I think we were in this forest, Debica-- not terribly far.
Occasionally, we would go into a farmer's house to sleep and get something to eat.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But that's how it was.
And if the luggage was too much, the suitcase was too much, well, we just left it and went ahead without it.
As the Germans were approaching, people always used to come, and soldiers on horses, telling us that the Germans are in such-and-such a place which was not too far from us.
Polish soldiers, yes.
Yes.
Now, when I look back upon it, I think they were running away, too.
That they didn't come to tell us, really, where the German soldiers were already.
But, more or less, running ahead, because there was no hope to fight.
All they had was a horse and a bayonet or szabla.
How is szabla in--
A sword, yes.
Maybe gun.
But they had guns.
They had tanks.
They were equipped extremely well.
So the poor Polish soldiers didn't stand a chance.
They were not equipped for war.
Long enough to get very, very sick.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because we ate whatever we could find, and wherever it was.
And, of course, very soon we developed cholera.
A lot of people suffered with cholera.
With us, we had also a few children from orphanage.
My parents were active in welfare society.
So the orphanage was divided amongst different people quickly, to take the children with us, and we did.
On the train back home, we have had the children with us.
And we had a little dog, which also was with us on the running trip.
And he did get infection, and he died.
It was a lovely little sausage dog.
So those little things were important, then.
Well, I liked very much to go with my mother to town.
And somehow, on many occasions-- there was a very old synagogue called the Remuh synagogue.

And when there was my sister exams or some other important business deal, my mother made a point of going to that Remuh synagogue, to the cemetery-- and there is a history attached to that cemetery-- and light a candle or yahrtzeit light.
I enjoyed-- yahrtzeit is a memorial light which you light after a dead person, or if you have a certain [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wish you ask God.
And in-- and this is like a-- not sacrifice, but you light the light to-- I guess so God would see the prayer better.
The wish would be granted.
Terrible thing to say that, isn't it?
But we were very much believers, certainly my mother.
It was in a great sincerity.
And I was always taken to that Remuh synagogue.
That was ?] most of our excursions.
Don't remember going to the pictures.
